facts.text: Kingston was founded in 1661 when the Dutch granted a charter, naming the settlement Wiltwijck before the English renamed it Kingston in 1669.
The city sits where Rondout Creek meets the Hudson River, 91 miles north of New York City and 59 miles south of Albany.
Kingston’s waterfront once thrived as a shipping hub, with the Delaware and Hudson Canal arriving in 1825 and later replaced by railroads.
The Reher Center for Immigrant Culture and History preserves a 20th‑century Jewish bakery that served rye, pumpernickel, and challah to working‑class immigrants.
Today, the Hudson River Maritime Museum and the Trolley Museum of New York interpret Kingston’s historic role as a transportation crossroads.
